739000.reason: This server manages HVAC equipment in several buildings throughout campus including labs, the library and other academic buildings. The buildings cannot be converted to another system without a large capitol investment as it requires replacing much of the HVAC equipment itself. The OS is end of life and will not receive updates. Excessive network traffic has caused the server to crash in the past and required a hard reboot. I believe that we already had an exception in place for this system but that was before we had a process for proper exception requests. It is likely unsupported by either the EDR nor vulnerability management agent software.
739000.riskAssessment: This system monitors and manages HVAC controls throughout many buildings on campus still. Loss of the system would prevent Facilities from being able to monitor those buildings or to adjust settings as needed.
739000.dataLevelStored: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)

739000.mitigation: Currently access is limited by the Palo Alto firewall to the Facilities BAS security zone. The server does not have internet access and is inaccessible from most of the UD network.

783482.reason:
The following list of machines in the Physics & Astronomy department will need Nessus agent exceptions.  The majority of them are older systems that are no longer in regular use, but must still be available for their storage access.  They will planned to be fully decomissioned in the coming year.    Some are on older hardware or are apart of an older cluster that would require significant amounts of funding and time from the owners of the systems in order to support an up-to-date operating system.
 
These machines do not contain any data above Level I.

attachments.extracted_text_preview:
Decommission and Exception strategy for aging Physics computational and
storage servers.
These following machines were part of the former Asterix cluster ran by the Ice Cube project in the 
Physics & Astronomy department.  
◦ Asterix-admin.physics.udel.edu
◦ asterix-login.physics.udel.edu
◦ asterix-io1.physics.udel.edu
◦ cacofonix.physics.udel.edu
◦ idefix.physics.udel.edu
Asterix-io1, cacofonix, and idefix are the three main storage servers of the cluster and currently house 
archived research data from past research projects that were conducted on the cluster.  Asterix-login 
and Asterix-admin are used to access the three storage servers over the network.  These systems can be 
further archived by shutting down each unit and removing from the network entirely as the need to 
access the archived data is slim.  
I will work with the remaining researcher that is accessing the data to plan a decommission date
by the end of October, 2024.
These following machines still have active use, however the challenging aspect about them is the 
hardware is long out of date and will not support a newer OS install and the owner’s of the systems do 
not have any prospects or funding to consider replacing them.
◦ Beatrice.physics.udel.edu
◦ stark.physics.udel.edu
◦ stcpc.bartol.udel.edu
◦ stcsun.bartol.udel.edu
◦ dante.bartol.udel.edu
◦ dante-io1.physics.udel.edu
◦ dante-io2.physics.udel.edu
The work being done on these machines could be moved or offloaded on to places such as Caviness by 
working closely and directly with each individual Faculty Member.  It may also come to be that the 
software in use will not function anywhere else and a great deal of push back could be expected.  
I intend to begin coordinating with each Faculty member starting in September 2024 to develop 
a move plan for the work and software to be migrated to a community cluster or other up-to-date 
system that they have access to.  
These following machines still have active use and have recent or still wort

783482.riskAssessment:
Machines are due to be decommissioned within the next year and will not be replaced.  
Some machines the hardware is far past End of Life and cannot support an up-to-date operating system to run the Nessus Agent.  These machines in particular are used by Faculty for their minimal research needs and no longer have major funding to support the systems.  

Some systems will require significant overhaul to have current operating system installed, and the age of the hardware does not justify the time investment.

783482.impactedSystems: Access to Research data and hardware to perform research for several Physics & Astronomy Research groups.  Graduate students could be unable to perform their course work or Thesis work towards earning their degree.
783482.mitigation: Systems are locally Firewall'd to only on campus access and are currently monitored by Physics & Astronomy Zabbix server to ensure health and integrity of the systems until they can be fully decommissioned.

797242.reason:
The bookstore is the sole entity responsible for emailing every student enrolled in the Digital Delivery Program (least expensive way to purchase course materials, that faculty choose to use) prior to the start of classes and prior to the end of add/drop for every session (Fall, Winter, Spring, Summer, as well as Online MBA sessions). We send emails based on rosters, so students are receiving one email for each Digital Delivery classes they have. This email is crucial access, billing, and opt out information. As an example we will send out approximately 23K twice for fall semester. We can only send 1,500 emails per day per email, taking us approx 6-7 days to complete.

Emails needing access are:
jgalt@udel.edu
lkillmey@udel.edu
Klatta@udel.edu
ericb@udel.edu

Last years policy approval ticket was 615947

Thank you,
Jennifer

814781.reason: The Wowza Streaming Engine is used by the UD Streamcast service to stream presentations to faculty, staff, and students of UD, as well as guests from all over the world. Port 1935 (TCP) is the port and the protocol that clients use to start the stream. It will need to be available from everywhere as long as the UD Streamcast service exists.
814781.riskAssessment: The Wowza Streaming Engine is used by the UD Streamcast service to stream presentations to faculty, staff, and students of UD, as well as guests from all over the world. Port 1935 (TCP) is the port that clients connect to to start the stream. It will need to be available from everywhere as long as the UD Streamcast service exists.
814781.dataLevelStored: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
814781.dataAccessLevel: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
814781.mitigation: Local firewall rules lock down all ports except port 1935, protocol TCP.

816080.reason:
Request Tracker is our ticketing system and uses email to receive/create tickets. Historically, this has been used by several groups within DAR and has been used with non-UD people. So until we can confirm there is no longer a need to receive email from any external email server, we need to keep this open for receiving emails on port 25.

We will review internally and determine if we can lock this down to just Google, Microsoft, and mail.udel.edu mail servers. We do have postfix configured to not work as an open relay. If there are any scans showing misconfiguration errors, please let us know and we'll resolve ASAP.
816080.riskAssessment: Critical for our ticketing system used by multiple DAR teams.
816080.dataLevelStored: Level II - Moderate impact: 'official use' information (eg., work emails, internal docs)
816080.dataAccessLevel: Level II - Moderate impact: 'official use' information (eg., work emails, internal docs)
816080.impactedSystems: Tickets for DAR units, includes non-public contact/donor information, supports our internal Tableau dashboards and intranet site (Portal - portal.dar.udel.edu) along with internal department processes and support of UD department information requests for donor lists
816080.mitigation: Tenable, local firewall rules are enforced for all other ports and limited to only on-campus or select on-campus/VPN IP addresses, patches are installed weekly

816094.reason:
The college of engineering has a mail server that is used to automatically generate mailing lists for CoE departments (Faculty, Staff, Researchers, Postdocs, Grad Students, Undergrad Students [Freshmen, Sophomore, Juniors, Seniors], etc) and has rules to automatically include/exclude specific individuals as needed. This system is firewalled off by CoE-IT to only allow specific mail servers to reach it. Emails are actually first send to a UD Google Group (to provide @udel.edu addresses) and those get sent to the CoE mail server. Upon arrival, the mail system checks that the header includes the correct Google Group ID number and then double checks that the mail sender is valid before attempting to process any mail.

As such, we need to keep the stmp ports open on this machine to ensure that timely notifications to end users.

This box also has an HTTPS web GUI that is only used on campus (behind CAS), that is used to modify exceptions lists and authenticated senders.
816094.riskAssessment: This is for automating emailing lists to ensure no student, faculty, nor staff member is missed in communications as the lists are generated in real time from nightly data feeds.
816094.dataLevelStored: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
816094.dataAccessLevel: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
816094.mitigation: Much of this has been noted above. We do use a top of rack firewall to limit inbound smtp access to only Google, MS Exchange, Comcast (done for testing purposes) mail servers. Crowdstrike has been on the existing server for some time.

876193.reason: Microscopy.dbi.udel.edu is a server storing archival data of the Bioimaging Core.  It is not open to the Internet (it used to be but this rule was removed from the UD firewall).  It runs a very old version of Linux -  Fedora 23.  No OS upgrade is available at this point. It would have to be a fresh new OS install. As it is a 13 year old hardware, there is a question of support in new versions of Linux.  Since the system is very static with almost no user activity (only a handful of users have smb login access rights), this does not seem to be worth the effort to bring it up to date, hence the request to leave it alone and do not scan it.
876193.riskAssessment: Type of data stored on the server is of very low risk. Access to the system is tightly controlled. The risk is minimal.

991719.reason:
GRC cannot approve phiredup. Since level III data is involved, we require third party security assessments. The vendor would not provide any third-party security assessments, so we are unable to conduct an accurate security assessment of the vendor. The department can move forward with the solution but would need to submit a security exception to accept the risk. I pulled their bitsight security score which was a 680, intermediate, medium risk of security incidents occurring. The PCI documentation was not signed by a QSA, so it is essentially a self-assessment.

Phired Up is used for Fraternity and Sorority Recruitment. It has been used for over a decade at University of Delaware and is used by hundreds of greek communities on colleges around the nation. The program is critical to run the matching process for sorority recruitment which includes thousands of students each year. We have a secure login to the site and have the ability to limit who has access to the site through the users section. We also can control the questions asked of students who register for recruitment and do NOT ask them and PII information. Contact information and UDID are provided to allow us to use university systems for all other verification and communication needs. We are in a niche area of higher education and the vendors are limited and also young companies with very small business approach, Phired Up has worked very hard with the university to provide the self assessment and answer all questions, third party assessments were cost prohibitive for them. 

NOTE END DATE: We use this program every year and would appreciate this exception lasting beyond this year, so that the work on everyone to maintain this service is reduced. For this academic year, our use of the service lasts until May 2026.

attachments.extracted_text_preview:
University of Delaware
Payment Card Process Exception Request Form

This form should be completed by a Department Head (or equivalent) to request an exception to the University of Delaware’s standard payment card acceptance equipment, payment gateways, processes, PCI DSS compliance documentation, merchant of record (MOR) requirements, and vendors.

The request will be reviewed by Treasury Services, in consultation with Arrow Payments, who will:
1) Assist the requester to ensure all information has been appropriately completed, as needed
2) Route the request for review and approval by the PCI DSS Compliance Advisory Committee
3) Track approved exceptions

Please complete all sections below

Date
Enter the date that the Exception Request Form was completed.
Your answer


Requester
Include the name, email, and phone number of the requester (IT director/service director/department head).
Your answer


Department/Collegiate Unit
Include the name of the department or unit requesting the exception.
Your answer


Risk Owner
Identify the name, email and phone number of the Department/Collegiate unit executive or IT Service Director responsible for the process.
Your answer


Exception Request Renewal
If you are requesting a renewal of a current exception, enter the exception request number of the original request.
Your answer


Exception Request
Payment Card Acceptance Equipment/Devices
Payment Gateways
Payment Card Acceptance Processes
PCI DSS Compliance Documentation
Merchant of Record (MOR) Requirement
Vendors

Explain in detail the exception that is being requested.
Your answer


Transaction Volume
Provide the estimated annual payment card transaction amounts for the requested exception.  Include estimated number of payment card transactions per year, as well as the estimated dollar amount of payment card transactions per year.
Your answer


Rationale/Business Justification
Explain why the requested exception is needed.
Your answer


Current Payment Card Acceptance Proces

991719.riskAssessment: Phired Up Campus Director software is critical for the sorority recruitment process. It runs the matching process and helps us navigate the placement of thousand potential new members into 12 sororities at university of delaware. Each year we use this system, and have for over a decade, there are thousands of members that rely on this program for recruitment. The company and software are used by hundreds of greek communities across the nation. There is only one other company on the market with a software that can also run sorority recruitment, we contacted them about their security but they too do not have a third party QSA. The other company has a lesser product and the lift to train hundreds of student leaders on a new software that doesn't integrate into their national organizations voting software would create a huge hurdle. Continuing to use this software is critical to our operations in recruitment to sustain our fraternity and sorority community.
991719.impactedSystems:
Data Level - 3, includes contact information for students who register, includes information which sororities would not want public such as their invitation lists. 
User Impact - Extensive, fraternity and sorority community is roughly 5000 students. 
University Impact - Mission Critical, we can not run sorority recruitment without this software.

997477.reason:
Several of our BMC/service processor interfaces have tripped the security scanners.

We interface with these mainly to provide an IPMI/server management interface to the servers at hand.  We already have these processors on the 10. private network, and would be willing to firewall them more strictly, e.g. each communication to each IP could be restricted to its own VLAN, or even a specific management host, TBD.
997477.riskAssessment: Patching/updating these endpoints is not feasible.  It would most not even be possible in some cases.  In other cases, it would require patching firmwares/BIOSes which is time consuming and error prone, bringing critical site resources down for too long (with a danger of indefinite downtime in the worst case).  I am also leaving the University and would not have time to patch these.
997477.dataLevelStored: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
997477.dataLevelStoredSpecified: Data with low confidentiality, integrity and availability concerns
997477.dataAccessLevel: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
997477.dataAccessLevelSpecified: Data with low confidentiality, integrity and availability concerns
997477.impactedSystems: These are not full systems with firewalls, OSs, etc.  They are peripheral devices to provide access to existing servers, which *are* patched regularly and maintained behind firewalls, etc.
997477.mitigation: We use encrypted credentials to log into these peripheral endpoints, without which a user (theoretically) could gain access to these BMC endpoints.  Even access to these endpoints does not confer access to the systems, as the logins for those would still be needed on the remote console.  The worst an attacker could do, with unmitigated access to these endpoints, is to view the system console and/or reboot or shut down the parent system(s).

1019629.reason:
The Library, Museums and Press has a group of computers  that are secured, 'frozen' using the Faronics Deep Freeze.  These computers are in areas accessible to the public after normal business hours, areas that can be minimally or unmonitored at certain times when Morris Library is still open.  Freezing them protects against tampering or  undesired changes.   Unfortunately freezing them also hinders, regular active updates since they are vulnerable to change while unfrozen, such as  for patching purposes.  Library IT has established two patching windows for these systems in between Fall and Spring semester.  All systems are fully patched before Fall or Spring semester begins.

Installing Tenable on these systems would just generate alerts that could not/would not be dealt with for months.  Given that we request an exception to installing the Tenable agent on these frozen systems.

Attached is a list of the 198 frozen systems in Morris Library, including loaner systems.  The list includes system names and ip addresses.  Loaner and instructional laptops are  wifi configured only
1019629.riskAssessment: These systems are protected by Crowdstrike and revert to their original state on reboot.  While it is valuable  to know potentially vulnerabilities, it is useless if not acted upon in a timely  fashion, and being frozen precludes this.  There is a much greater risk of tampering and exploits if these computers were not frozen.
1019629.impactedSystems: Public,  loaner, and public service desk client systems.  Level 1 data only.  No firewall exceptions.  Low impact.  Non-critical.

1019927.reason:
The laptops will be used in a Pod (clean room) at our Blackhawk facility. The Pod does not have wired network access. At present, there are cost constraints that preclude adding network access to the Pods. The laptop will be connected via USB to process equipment and will run the equipment software and collect research data. The data will be transferred to USB drive for removal from the clean room for further analysis on NIIMBL managed equipment. The laptop will be setup as follows:
  • Auto login to desktop (non-admin account)
  • WiFi disabled in BIOS (they should not be connected to any network)
  • BitLocker enabled (keys stored in BitWarden)
  • No domain join, or any other NIIMBL/UD software.
In short, the laptop will be stand-alone and thus will not be domain joined or run any management or security agents.
1019927.riskAssessment: Laptops are essential for operating and interfacing with laboratory equipment in the Clean Room
1019927.dataLevelStored: Level II - Moderate impact: 'official use' information (eg., work emails, internal docs)
1019927.dataAccessLevel: Level II - Moderate impact: 'official use' information (eg., work emails, internal docs)
1019927.impactedSystems: The data will be transferred to USB drive for removal from the clean room for further analysis on NIIMBL managed equipment.
1019927.mitigation: The data will be transferred to USB drive for removal from the clean room for further analysis on NIIMBL managed equipment.

1024559.reason:
donbot.dbi.udel.edu -10.2.123.89 is a Vmware ESXi server. It hosts three VMs which are still in use: PaperCut server, FlexLM license manager, and a Win server instance.  Moving them to a different platform would be difficult, and very disruptive.  FlexLM licenses are tied up to the actual hardware. They cannot be migrated, they have to be reissued. Similar issue with PaperCut.  

Can't we leave this system alone?  It resides quitely on 10.2 subnet. It does not bother anyone. There are no user accounts on it. The risk is minimal, if any at all. 

Moving the above services to a different platform in in our long term - such as 12 months - plans. This will be either another virtualization platform, such as Proxmox, or individual physical servers.  But please just leave it alone for the time being.
1024559.riskAssessment:
Most of the reasons and impact have been described above. To repeat myself here: The server hosts three VMs which: PaperCut server, FlexLM license manager, and a Win server instance.  Moving them to a different platform would be difficult, and very disruptive.  FlexLM licenses are tied up to the actual hardware. They cannot be migrated, they have to be reissued. Similar issue with PaperCut.  We cannot afford any long term interruptions to these services.
The risk is minimal, if any at all. There are no user accounts on the server in question, just a couple of admin level accounts protected by secure passwords. Also, there are no higher level user data. There is really nothing of value there.
1024559.impactedSystems: These are non-critical no risk data. Absolutely nothing of value.

1051672.reason:
The firewalls for servers login00.darwin.hpc.udel.edu and login01.darwin.hpc.udel.edu should allow traffic from owell.nss.udel.edu and nwell.nss.udel.edu through ports 7937–9936 with TCP conntections. This is necessary for creating backups of the darwin login nodes.

Please let me know if you have any further questions or concerns.
1051672.riskAssessment: Required for establishing system backups. This will help with BCP.
